texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Dynamics of a mean-reverting stochastic volatility equation with regime switching
texts.text: Dynamics of a mean-reverting stochastic volatility equation with regime switching

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yanling Zhu a , Kai Wang a,b, ∗ and Yong Ren c, ∗
texts.text: Yanling Zhu a , Kai Wang a,b, ∗ and Yong Ren c, ∗

texts.label: text
texts.orig: a Department of Applied Mathematics, Anhui University of Finance and Economics, Bengbu 233030, CHINA b Center for Applied Mathematics, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, CHINA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Abstract: In this paper, we consider a mean-reverting stochastic volatility equation with regime switching, and present some sufficient conditions for the existence of global positive solution, asymptotic boundedness in p th moment, positive recurrence and existence of stationary distribution of this equation. Some results obtained in this paper extend the ones in literature. Example is given to verify the results by simulation.
texts.text: Abstract: In this paper, we consider a mean-reverting stochastic volatility equation with regime switching, and present some sufficient conditions for the existence of global positive solution, asymptotic boundedness in p th moment, positive recurrence and existence of stationary distribution of this equation. Some results obtained in this paper extend the ones in literature. Example is given to verify the results by simulation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Mean-reverting stochastic volatility equation; Global positive solution; Asymptotic boundedness in p th moment; Positive recurrence; Stationary distribution
texts.text: Keywords: Mean-reverting stochastic volatility equation; Global positive solution; Asymptotic boundedness in p th moment; Positive recurrence; Stationary distribution

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Stochastic volatility means that volatility is not a constant, but a stochastic process. By assuming that the volatility of the underlying price is a stochastic process rather than a constant, it becomes possible to more accurately model derivatives. Mean-reverting stochastic volatility model is used in the fields of quantitative finance and financial engineering to evaluate derivative securities, such as options and swaps. The general mean-reverting stochastic volatility model can be expressed by the following equation:
texts.text: Stochastic volatility means that volatility is not a constant, but a stochastic process. By assuming that the volatility of the underlying price is a stochastic process rather than a constant, it becomes possible to more accurately model derivatives. Mean-reverting stochastic volatility model is used in the fields of quantitative finance and financial engineering to evaluate derivative securities, such as options and swaps. The general mean-reverting stochastic volatility model can be expressed by the following equation:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: dX t = ( a -bX t ) dt + σX θ t dB t , (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where X t presents the price or the variance of the price returns of a stock, a, b are positive constants, σ is the volatility rate, θ is a nonnegative constant, B t is a Brownian motion defined on a complete filtration probability space (Ω , F , {F t } t ⩾ 0 , P ), and the filtration {F t } t ⩾ 0 satisfies the usual condition. Equation (1) has important application in economy, such as the variance of the price returns of stock and the option price(cf. [1]). For examples, when θ = 0, it reduces to the mean-reverting Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model, Mao (1997) [2] discussed the limit distribution of its solution as time t goes to infinity; when θ = 1 / 2, it is the square root stochastic variance model, which was used by Cox, Ingersoll and Ross (1985) [3] to express the dynamics of interest rate, and by Heston (1993) [4] to investigate the pricing of a European call option on an asset with stochastic volatility, and as the exchange rate processes by Bates (1996) [5] to investigate Deutsche mark options; while θ = 1, it transforms to the Garch Diffusion model, and as θ ⩾ 1 / 2 Model (1) reduces to the Constant Elasticity of Variance model, which provides a relatively simple illustration of many of the effects of volatility explosions; these two stochastic models were presented in Ghysels, Harvey and Renault (1996) [6] for studying the stochastic volatility in financial markets. For the general case of θ ∈ [1 / 2 , + ∞ ), Mao (1997) [2] shows its solution X t > 0 for all t > 0 almost surely.
texts.text: where X t presents the price or the variance of the price returns of a stock, a, b are positive constants, σ is the volatility rate, θ is a nonnegative constant, B t is a Brownian motion defined on a complete filtration probability space (Ω , F , {F t } t ⩾ 0 , P ), and the filtration {F t } t ⩾ 0 satisfies the usual condition. Equation (1) has important application in economy, such as the variance of the price returns of stock and the option price(cf. [1]). For examples, when θ = 0, it reduces to the mean-reverting Ornstein-Uhlenbeck model, Mao (1997) [2] discussed the limit distribution of its solution as time t goes to infinity; when θ = 1 / 2, it is the square root stochastic variance model, which was used by Cox, Ingersoll and Ross (1985) [3] to express the dynamics of interest rate, and by Heston (1993) [4] to investigate the pricing of a European call option on an asset with stochastic volatility, and as the exchange rate processes by Bates (1996) [5] to investigate Deutsche mark options; while θ = 1, it transforms to the Garch Diffusion model, and as θ ⩾ 1 / 2 Model (1) reduces to the Constant Elasticity of Variance model, which provides a relatively simple illustration of many of the effects of volatility explosions; these two stochastic models were presented in Ghysels, Harvey and Renault (1996) [6] for studying the stochastic volatility in financial markets. For the general case of θ ∈ [1 / 2 , + ∞ ), Mao (1997) [2] shows its solution X t > 0 for all t > 0 almost surely.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As we know that in the real world, the socio-economic environment is constantly changing, which means that the parameters a, b, σ and θ in equation (1) will change as the social and economic environment changes. This type of noise caused by the change of economic environment is often called telegraph noise, which can be demonstrated as a switching between two or more regimes of states. The continuous time Markov chain models the regime switching well. Kazangey and Sworder (1971) [7] presented a switching system, where a macroeconomic model of the national economy was used to study the effect of federal housing removal policies on the stabilization of the housing sector. The term describing the influence of interest rates was modelled by a finite-state Markov chain to provide a quantitative measure of the effect of interest rate uncertainty on optimal policy. Readers can see Mao and Yuan (2006) [9], and Yin and Zhu (2009) [10] for more details on the theory of switching systems, which are two excellent references on this subject.
texts.text: As we know that in the real world, the socio-economic environment is constantly changing, which means that the parameters a, b, σ and θ in equation (1) will change as the social and economic environment changes. This type of noise caused by the change of economic environment is often called telegraph noise, which can be demonstrated as a switching between two or more regimes of states. The continuous time Markov chain models the regime switching well. Kazangey and Sworder (1971) [7] presented a switching system, where a macroeconomic model of the national economy was used to study the effect of federal housing removal policies on the stabilization of the housing sector. The term describing the influence of interest rates was modelled by a finite-state Markov chain to provide a quantitative measure of the effect of interest rate uncertainty on optimal policy. Readers can see Mao and Yuan (2006) [9], and Yin and Zhu (2009) [10] for more details on the theory of switching systems, which are two excellent references on this subject.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our contributions in this paper are as follows.
texts.text: Our contributions in this paper are as follows.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We show the existence of global almost surely positive solution to equation (2) for any initial value ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + ×M , where R + = (0 , ∞ ), which extends the corresponding result in Mao et al. [11].
texts.text: We show the existence of global almost surely positive solution to equation (2) for any initial value ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + ×M , where R + = (0 , ∞ ), which extends the corresponding result in Mao et al. [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: · We obtain the estimation of the p th moment, asymptotic boundedness in p th moment and the Lyapunov exponent of the solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) to equation (2).
texts.text: · We obtain the estimation of the p th moment, asymptotic boundedness in p th moment and the Lyapunov exponent of the solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) to equation (2).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · We present some sufficient conditions for that the process ( X t , r t ) determined by equation (2) is positive recurrence and admits a unique ergodic asymptotically invariant distribution.
texts.text: We present some sufficient conditions for that the process ( X t , r t ) determined by equation (2) is positive recurrence and admits a unique ergodic asymptotically invariant distribution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Global positive solution
texts.text: 2 Global positive solution

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 2.1. If for all i ∈ M , θ ( i ) = 1 / 2 and 2 a ( i ) ⩾ σ 2 ( i ) ; or θ ( i ) > 1 / 2 , then for any ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + ×M , there is a unique solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) to equation (2) on t ⩾ 0 and this solution also satisfies P ( X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) > 0 , ∀ t ⩾ 0) = 1 .
texts.text: Theorem 2.1. If for all i ∈ M , θ ( i ) = 1 / 2 and 2 a ( i ) ⩾ σ 2 ( i ) ; or θ ( i ) > 1 / 2 , then for any ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + ×M , there is a unique solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) to equation (2) on t ⩾ 0 and this solution also satisfies P ( X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) > 0 , ∀ t ⩾ 0) = 1 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 2.1. If # M = 1 ( M has only one state), that is, there is no regime switching in equation (2) , Mao et al. [2] investigated the global existence of positive solution to it for the case of θ ⩾ 1 / 2 . For the regime switching equation (2) with θ ( r t ) ≡ θ , Mao et al. [11] proved the existence of global positive solution to it for the case of θ ∈ [1 / 2 , 1] . For the case of # M > 1 , that is, the regime switching equation (2) , Theorem 2.1 presents the same results for θ ( r t ) ∈ [1 / 2 , 1] . Moreover, one can see that Theorem 2.1 extends the existence results to the case of θ ( r t ) > 1 . For the case of θ ( r t ) ∈ (0 , 1 / 2) , pathwise uniqueness does not hold, see Girsanov [12]; and for θ ( r t ) ≡ 0 , the solution of equation (2) can be expressed explicitly, which shows that it can be negative.
texts.text: Remark 2.1. If # M = 1 ( M has only one state), that is, there is no regime switching in equation (2) , Mao et al. [2] investigated the global existence of positive solution to it for the case of θ ⩾ 1 / 2 . For the regime switching equation (2) with θ ( r t ) ≡ θ , Mao et al. [11] proved the existence of global positive solution to it for the case of θ ∈ [1 / 2 , 1] . For the case of # M > 1 , that is, the regime switching equation (2) , Theorem 2.1 presents the same results for θ ( r t ) ∈ [1 / 2 , 1] . Moreover, one can see that Theorem 2.1 extends the existence results to the case of θ ( r t ) > 1 . For the case of θ ( r t ) ∈ (0 , 1 / 2) , pathwise uniqueness does not hold, see Girsanov [12]; and for θ ( r t ) ≡ 0 , the solution of equation (2) can be expressed explicitly, which shows that it can be negative.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. For the case of θ ∈ [1 / 2 , 1), the coefficients of equation (2) obey H¨ o lder continuity and linear growth condition on (0 , + ∞ ); and for the case of θ ⩾ 1, the coefficients of equation (2) are locally Lipschitz continuous on (0 , + ∞ ). Thus for any given initial value ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + × M there is a unique maximal local solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) on [0 , τ e ), where τ e is the explosion time, see Ikeda [8] for more details on the uniqueness of the solution to stochastic differential equation with H¨ o lder continuous coefficients.
texts.text: Proof. For the case of θ ∈ [1 / 2 , 1), the coefficients of equation (2) obey H¨ o lder continuity and linear growth condition on (0 , + ∞ ); and for the case of θ ⩾ 1, the coefficients of equation (2) are locally Lipschitz continuous on (0 , + ∞ ). Thus for any given initial value ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + × M there is a unique maximal local solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) on [0 , τ e ), where τ e is the explosion time, see Ikeda [8] for more details on the uniqueness of the solution to stochastic differential equation with H¨ o lder continuous coefficients.

texts.orig: Now we show that the solution is globally existent and positive on R + a.s.. Let k 0 > 0 be sufficiently large such that x 0 ∈ [1 /k 0 , k 0 ], and for each integer k ⩾ k 0 define the stopping time τ k = inf { t ∈ [0 , τ e ) | X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) ̸∈ (1 /k, k ) } , where τ ∞ = lim k →∞ τ k . It is obvious that τ ∞ ⩽ τ e . So we only need to show that τ ∞ = ∞ a.s., which yields the positiveness of the solution almost surely and the existence of global solution of equation (2). If it is false, then there is a pair of constants T > 0 and ǫ ∈ (0 , 1) such that P { τ ∞ ⩽ T } > ǫ, which yields that there exists an integer k 1 ⩾ k 0 such that

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 p th Moment Estimation
texts.text: 3 p th Moment Estimation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we will give the p th moment estimation of the solution to equation (2), and then present some sufficient conditions for asymptotic boundedness in p th moment of the solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ).
texts.text: In this section, we will give the p th moment estimation of the solution to equation (2), and then present some sufficient conditions for asymptotic boundedness in p th moment of the solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3.1. (Mao and Yuan (2006) [9])
texts.text: Definition 3.1. (Mao and Yuan (2006) [9])

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A square matrix A is called a nonsingular M-matrix if A can be expressed in the form A = sI -G with some G ⩾ 0 (that is each element of G is non-nagative) and s > ρ ( G ) , where I is the identity matrix and ρ ( G ) the spectral radius of G .
texts.text: A square matrix A is called a nonsingular M-matrix if A can be expressed in the form A = sI -G with some G ⩾ 0 (that is each element of G is non-nagative) and s > ρ ( G ) , where I is the identity matrix and ρ ( G ) the spectral radius of G .

texts.text: 2) ∀ i ∈ M , θ ( i ) ∈ [0 , 1] , and A ( p ) is a nonsingular M -matrix with p > max { 1 , 2[1 -min i ∈M θ ( i )] } . Then the p th moment of the solution to equation (2) has the following property,

texts.label: formula

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 Example
texts.text: 5 Example

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we will give an example to verify the theorems obtained in previous sections by simulation. The numerical method used here is Milsteins Higher Order Method, see Higham (2001) [14] for more details. Set the state space of Markov chain ( r t ) t ⩾ 0 as M = { 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 } , and its generator Q as follows
texts.text: In this section, we will give an example to verify the theorems obtained in previous sections by simulation. The numerical method used here is Milsteins Higher Order Method, see Higham (2001) [14] for more details. texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6 Conclusion
texts.text: 6 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we investigate a general stochastic volatility equation with regime switching, and show that for any initial value ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + ×M there is a unique global almost surely positive solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) to this equation; and give the p th moment estimation of the solution by using the properties of nonsingular M -matrix, and present some simple sufficient conditions for the existence of a unique ergodic stationary distribution of the equation.
texts.text: In this paper, we investigate a general stochastic volatility equation with regime switching, and show that for any initial value ( x 0 , i 0 ) ∈ R + ×M there is a unique global almost surely positive solution X t ( x 0 , i 0 ) to this equation; and give the p th moment estimation of the solution by using the properties of nonsingular M -matrix, and present some simple sufficient conditions for the existence of a unique ergodic stationary distribution of the equation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It will be more interesting if the Markov chain ( r t ) t ⩾ 0 is state dependent or infinity, also the equation with jumps will be better than the one without jumps in describing some complicated dynamics behaviors in the real world.
texts.text: It will be more interesting if the Markov chain ( r t ) t ⩾ 0 is state dependent or infinity, also the equation with jumps will be better than the one without jumps in describing some complicated dynamics behaviors in the real world.
